meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Motion of Connected Block Over Pulley (Atwood Machine)

intro.kicker: Topic 19 of 22 • Chapter: Newton’s Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Motion of Connected Block Over Pulley (Atwood Machine)
intro.description: The Atwood machine is one of the most tested NEET setups: two masses m₁ and m₂ connected by a string over a frictionless, massless pulley. The heavier mass descends, lighter ascends. Key results: acceleration a = (m₂−m₁)g/(m₁+m₂) and tension T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂). Extended to 3-mass pulley systems, these formulas remain the anchor for ≥4 NEET questions over every 6-year exam cycle.

expectedQuestions.description: The Atwood machine formula is a NEET fast-win. Direct substitution MCQs appear 1–2 times per paper. The 3-mass extension appears sporadically.

timeRequired.description: Two-mass derivation from FBDs — 25 min; memorise and verify formulas — 10 min; three-mass system — 20 min; PYQ drills — 5 min.

difficulty.description: Two-mass Atwood is straightforward once the FBD equations are written correctly. The error zone is sign convention: which mass goes up vs down. Three-mass variants with compound pulleys are harder.

nriUsaGap.description: Atwood machine is taught in US physics courses. The tension formula T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂) is standard. US students may be less practiced on the 3-mass compound pulley extension.

bulletNotes: a = (m₂−m₁)g/(m₁+m₂) and T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂) are two of the most recalled NEET mechanics formulas.
Limiting cases: a = g when m₁ = 0 (free fall); T = 0 when m₁ → 0; T = m₂g when m₁ = m₂ (static case).
NEET sometimes asks for the tension in the fixed support: this equals 2T.

strategySteps.title: Derive from FBD — Never Memorise Blindly
strategySteps.description: Draw FBDs for m₁ (going up) and m₂ (going down). For m₁: T − m₁g = m₁a. For m₂: m₂g − T = m₂a. Add the two equations to eliminate T, giving a = (m₂−m₁)g/(m₁+m₂).

strategySteps.title: Memorise T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂)
strategySteps.description: This harmonic-mean-like formula gives the tension. Substitute into either FBD equation to verify. Note T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂) is always less than the weight of either mass when m₁ ≠ 0.

strategySteps.title: Three-Mass Compound System
strategySteps.description: When a third mass m₃ hangs from m₂ (or from the pulley axle via T₂), write separate FBD equations for each mass. T₁ and T₂ satisfy separate Newton’s 2nd Law equations per block.

cards.title: Atwood Machine — Complete Notes
cards.description: Two-mass FBD derivations, formula sheet (a, T, support tension), three-mass extension table, and NEET solved numericals covering all limiting cases.

subtopics.name: Two-Mass Atwood — Derivation of a and T

cards.title: Two-Mass Atwood — Core Derivation
cards.coreRevision: Two masses m₁ < m₂ connected over frictionless pulley. m₂ descends, m₁ ascends with common acceleration a. FBD for m₂: m₂g − T = m₂a. FBD for m₁: T − m₁g = m₁a. Adding: a = (m₂−m₁)g/(m₁+m₂).
points: When m₁ = m₂: a = 0, T = m₁g = m₂g (static)

cards.title: Tension in Fixed Support (Pulley Axle)
cards.coreRevision: The pulley support bears the combined pull of both string segments, each carrying tension T. Total tension on support = T₁ + T₂ = 2T = 4m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂). This is sometimes confused with T alone.
points: NEET sometimes asks for ‘force exerted on the ceiling’ — this is T₂
cards.example: m₁ = 3 kg, m₂ = 5 kg: Support = 2 × 37.5 = 75 N. Not 37.5 N.

cards.title: Three-Mass System
cards.coreRevision: Third mass m₃ hangs from m₂ (via T₂). Now the heavier side is m₂+m₃ combined. Acceleration a = [(m₂+m₃)−m₁]g/(m₁+m₂+m₃). T₁ and T₂ are derived separately from their respective FBD equations.
cards.example: Write separate Newton’s equations for m₁ (going up) and for m₂, m₃ (both going down) to get T₁ and T₂.

curriculum.hintText: Atwood machine is standard US AP Physics content. Compound pulley extensions and the support tension question are potentially new challenges.

gaps.title: Support (Fixed) Tension
gaps.description: US AP Physics students are often taught to compute T in the string but rarely asked for the tension in the pulley’s support (which is 2T for a fixed pulley). NEET targets this directly.
bullets: Remember: fixed pulley redirects force but does not reduce it. Each string leg carries T, so support carries 2T.
For a movable pulley (different mechanism), force is halved — but the Atwood machine uses a fixed pulley.

gaps.title: Three-Mass Compound Pulley
gaps.description: Textbooks in the US focus the Atwood machine at two masses. The three-mass variant with a string-of-strings configuration is predominantly tested in Indian competitive exams.
bullets: Draw one FBD per mass; write Newton’s 2nd Law for each; solve simultaneously.
Verify: when m₃ = 0, the result should reduce to the standard two-mass Atwood formula.

questions.question: In an Atwood machine, masses 3 kg and 7 kg are connected over a massless frictionless pulley. What is the acceleration of the masses? (g = 10 m/s²)
In the above Atwood machine (3 kg and 7 kg), what is the tension in the string? (g = 10 m/s²)
Atwood machine with m₁ = 2 kg, m₂ = 8 kg. Find the force on the pulley axle (support). (g = 10 m/s²)
In an Atwood machine, when would the string tension equal the weight of either mass?

items.title: Derive FBD equations for both masses
items.description: m₂g − T = m₂a (descending mass); T − m₁g = m₁a (ascending mass). Confirm sign convention consistently.

items.title: Solve simultaneously for a and T
items.description: Add the two FBD equations to eliminate T and get a. Substitute a back into either equation to get T.

items.title: Check S2T = support tension
items.description: If question asks for force on the pulley ceiling or axle, the answer is 2T, not T.

items.title: Verify limiting cases
items.description: m₁ = m₂ → a = 0, T = mg. m₁ = 0 → a = g, T = 0. These sanity checks confirm your derived formula.

items.question: Why is the Atwood machine tension less than either weight?
items.answer: If m₂ were in free fall, it would have no support and f = m₂g. But it is constrained by the string, which partially holds it back. The tension is less than m₂g (because m₂ is accelerating, not at rest) and greater than m₁g (because m₁ is accelerating upward, more than just supported). Result: m₁g < T < m₂g.

items.question: Why does a = (m₂-m₁)g/(m₁+m₂) make intuitive sense?
items.answer: The net driving force is the weight difference (m₂-m₁)g. The total mass being accelerated is (m₁+m₂). Newton’s 2nd Law directly gives a = net force / total mass. When m₂ = m₁, net force = 0, so a = 0 — the system is in equilibrium.

items.question: What is the effect of the pulley’s mass in a real Atwood machine?
items.answer: A massive pulley has rotational inertia, which reduces the acceleration. This effect is covered in Rotational Motion (Chapter 7), not in the Newton’s Laws treatment. For NEET, the pulley is always assumed massless and frictionless unless stated otherwise.

grounding_summary: Page 199 contains the Atwood machine Table with two-mass and three-mass configurations including equations T₁ = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂) and a = (m₂-m₁)g/(m₁+m₂).
The OCR confirms entry T₂ = 4m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂) for the pulley support tension (2T).
Research confirms the Atwood machine is the single highest-frequency Newton’s Law application in NEET with 1.5 questions per year average.
grounding_excerpt: Motion of Connected Block Over A Pulley
Motion of Blocks Connected by Mass Less String

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: NCERT derives the Atwood machine result as a worked example; confirms both a and T formulas.

sources.title: NEET UG 2026 Chapter-Wise Physics Strategy (Edufever) — Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: Identifies pulleys and tension as the highest-yield NEET topics within Laws of Motion; advises memorisation of Atwood formula as preparation priority.

review.notes: Content grounded in pages 198–199 covering Atwood machine and three-mass extension tables. Formulas verified from OCR table: a = [(m2-m1)/(m1+m2)]g; T = 2m1m2g/(m1+m2); T_support = 2T.
